setting.note: In 1327 the Franciscan friar William of Baskerville and his young novice Adso arrive at a rich Benedictine abbey in the mountains of northern Italy, where a series of deaths draws them into a labyrinthine library and a deadly secret about a forbidden book. Eco sets his medieval murder mystery at an unnamed monastery in the Ligurian Apennines: the scriptorium where the monks illuminate manuscripts, the great fortress-library of the Aedificium, the church and grounds, and the Inquisition that arrives to twist the search for truth.

rights.originalWork: In copyright. Umberto Eco, first published 1980 (Il nome della rosa). Mapped as a cited work: no text stored, no passages reproduced. The places are facts and the notes are our own.
rights.textSource: None stored. A cited book reproduces no text.
rights.territoryNotes: Locations and original commentary only. No reproduction of the copyrighted text, in any territory.
rights.attribution: The Name of the Rose, Umberto Eco (1980). Locations mapped by Read the Places; not affiliated with or endorsed by the author's estate or publisher.

palette.note: The gold-leaf of the illuminated manuscripts the abbey guards and copies, the treasure and the danger at the heart of the mystery. A cited book's colour is not a quotation, because we store no text to quote; it still has to be legible, and this clears WCAG AA on paper.
